summary: UR176 is a FULL MURDER DOSSIER from Chuquitanta (Chillon Valley north of Lima), dated February 1519 CE (3 years before Pizarro's landing). A mother (mama, on VR = vermilion / blood) was slaughtered -- PASS-chaqa, the khipu's first word, in BG identity colour. The entire case file hangs from cord 1 as a REVERSED TREE: 58 elements across 4 depth levels (L1 to L4), twenty-seven numbers and thirty-one words, co-occurring Quechua taka and Aymara chata for 'to strike' as if the clerk wanted no linguistic ambiguity. The falcon (wama x3) crosses all color registers as the accused: W (investigation), VR (blood, with chapa guardian), W again at the verdict. The sentence is written in the ONLY GG (justice green-yellow) cords of the document: taka + DIR-ta = 'strike toward'. Date: cord 63 wata (year) labels cord 65 INT=81 (1438 + 81 = 1519 CE); cord 66 chilla / killa first knot L2 = month 2 = Paucar Waray (February). 154 cords, 10 colors, 4 depth levels, 92.1 percent coverage. Most formally elaborated judicial khipu in the corpus.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  154 cords: 78 INT (sum=6850), 76 STRING, 0 EMPTY.
  4 clusters, 4 depth levels (L1-L4), paired architecture.
  American Museum of Natural History, New York.
  Provenance: Chuquitanta, Chillon Valley, north of Lima.
  Urton field note: spiral-type long knots; all single knots read as 10.
  Coverage: 92.1 percent (70/76 STRING cords confirmed).
  10 colors: W(107), BG(26), VR(6), KB(4), GG(4), AB(3), PB(1), VG(1), RG(1), ?(1).

COLOR CODE -- TRIAL PROCEDURE (validated in corpus)
  BG (blue-green, identity)        = the parties. Victim (PASS-chaqa), huaca, administrator (wapa),
                                     guardian (chapa), herd (tama), father (ACC-papa). 26 cords.
  GG (green-yellow, justice)       = the SENTENCE ONLY -- taka + DIR-ta. 4 cords.
  VR (vermilion, blood)            = mama (victim), wama (accused), associated numbers. 6 cords.
  W  (white, data)                 = amounts, dates, damages. 107 cords.
  KB (dark brown, data)            = secondary evidence -- taka, PASS-wa. 4 cords.

STRUCTURE
  CORD 1 -- the full case as a reversed tree (58 elements, 4 levels):
    L1: PASS-chaqa [BG] -- SLAUGHTERED (passive). The first word of the khipu.
    L2-L4 subsidiaries:
      ACC-pi               -- who? Investigation opens.
      waka [BG]            -- huaca. Murder near a sacred site.
      GEN-chata [BG]       -- strike (Aymara).
      taka [KB]            -- strike (Quechua). Two languages.
      wapa [BG]            -- administrator (investigating authority).
      wama [W] at L3       -- falcon (first appearance).
      tipi                 -- to tear out (additional violence).
      mama [VR] at L3      -- MOTHER in red. The victim.
      ACC-papa [BG]        -- father (witness / accomplice).
      wama [VR]            -- falcon in RED (second appearance).
      chapa [BG] at L3     -- guardian (linked to the falcon).
      tata                 -- father.
      tama [BG] at L3      -- herd (the motive?).
    Sum of INT in cord 1 subtree: 2384.

  INVESTIGATION (cords 2-22):
    Interrogatives hammer: GEN-pi, pinaka, pipa -- who? who? who?
    chana (cord 15) -- the CANAL (dried canal at the edge of Chuquitanta, crime scene).
    kusi (cord 19) -- 'joy / happiness' in Quechua. After a murder, this is a PROPER NAME
                       (Kusi Yupanki, Kusi Rimay are attested given names).
    mama-GEN-INTER (cord 20) -- 'of the mother, who?' Establishes maternal filiation = ayllu membership.
    1OBJ-pina-REFL (cord 22) -- 'me, who, myself.' The accused speaks.

  THE ACT (cords 23-37):
    223 (cord 23) -- largest number in the body (fine? distance?).
    chay, PASS-papa, chayki, pata -- 'this', 'the father passive', 'this yours', 'terrain'.
    The disputed property, a land dispute, likely motive.
    piy (cord 32) + sipi (cord 33) -- WHO + TO KILL on consecutive cords. Question and crime.
    mama-PASS (cord 36) -- 'the mother passive.' She SUFFERED. Confirmation: victim is a woman, a mother.

  THE ACCOUNTS (cords 38-68):
    11 consecutive INT cords (40-50) -- damages, compensation, blood price.
    chama (cord 51) -- food. tama (cord 54) -- herd.
    ACC-chapa (cord 61) -- guardian.
    wata (cord 63)   -- YEAR, text label.
    chilla (cord 66) -- KILLA / moon / month, text label.
    1OBJ-ku (cord 68) -- 'myself.' Signature.

  DATE EXTRACTION -- FEBRUARY 1519 CE
    Date embedded MID-TEXT (not at standard Mode A opening).
    Cord 63: wata (year)                 -- text label. Knots: L7 S10 L5.
    Cord 65: INT = 81                    -- S*7 + L11 = 81. Year = 1438 + 81 = 1519 CE.
    Cord 66: chilla (killa / month)      -- text label. First knot L2 (kvt = 2) -> month 2.
    Month 2 = Paucar Waray (February, feast of flowering).
    Preferred reading: February 1519 CE (late empire, Huayna Qhapaq reign).

  VERDICT (cords 84-96):
    PASS-wa      -- passive, me.
    wama (85)    -- falcon (THIRD and final appearance).
    taka [GG] (86) -- strike, in JUSTICE COLOUR. Only 4 GG cords in whole khipu.
    DIR-ta [GG] (87) -- toward. Direction of punishment.
    paka (92)    -- the SECRET.
    taka (93)    -- strike. The secret is STRUCK OPEN = revealed.
    Three closing numbers finalise the case.

THE FALCON (wama)
  Three appearances across three color registers:
    1. W at L3 in the case tree            -- investigation
    2. VR at L2 (with chapa guardian)       -- blood
    3. W in the verdict, before GG taka     -- condemned
  The falcon crosses evidence, blood, and justice -- the accused's epithet or code name.
  Andean naming used animal names commonly.

COMPARISON WITH UR1091
  UR1091 speaks of death in periphrases: tana mana...llapa ('the support is no more, divine lightning').
  UR176 says it directly: PASS-chaqa + sipi ('slaughtered' + 'to kill').
  Two registers of judicial khipu: bureaucratic euphemism vs judicial fact.
narrative:
A woman was slaughtered. That is the first word of the khipu.

PASS-chaqa -- slaughtered, in the passive voice, knotted in blue-green, the color of identity. Not a story, not an invocation, not a formula. A fact. Someone is dead, and this cotton thread says so before anything else.

The cord that carries this word is also the heaviest in the khipu. Fifty-eight elements hang from it -- twenty-seven numbers and thirty-one words, across four levels of depth. The complete case file, hanging from a single thread like a reversed tree. At the top, the murder. In the branches, the evidence.

The tree descends into the investigation. Who? The huaca is named -- the murder happened near a sacred site. Two words for the act: chata, to strike in Aymara, and taka, to strike in Quechua. Two languages, as if the clerk wanted no one to claim they did not understand.

The administrator investigates. And the falcon enters.

Wama -- the falcon. Three times it appears, each time at a critical point. First in the case tree, tied to an identity number. Second in red -- vermilion, blood -- just above the guardian. Third at the end, just before the sentence. The falcon is the predator. Perhaps the accused's nickname -- the Incas commonly used animal names as personal epithets -- or the symbol of a hunt gone wrong. It crosses the entire dossier like a shadow.

In the tree, the mother appears: mama, in red. The only red cord with a kinship word. In the khipu's color system, red marks blood. The mother is the victim.

The investigation continues in the body of the khipu. Questions hammer: who? who? who? A canal is named -- chana, the dried canal of Chuquitanta, in the Chillon Valley north of Lima. That is where the body was found.

Then a strange word: kusi. Joy. Happiness. After a slaughter, it is not an adjective -- it is a name. Kusi. Someone's name. A common Quechua given name -- Kusi Yupanki, Kusi Rimay -- that has become evidence.

The clerk asks: of the mother, who? In the Inca world maternal filiation determines ayllu membership. The question is not sentimental -- it is juridical. Where do you come from? Who will be responsible for the blood price?

The facts are laid out. The number 223 -- the largest in the body. The terrain, the father, this is yours. A contested plot, a land dispute perhaps, the cause of the murder. Then the two words side by side: piy -- who? sipi -- to kill. The question and the crime are one.

Mama-PASS -- the mother in the passive. She SUFFERED. The confirmation: the victim is a woman, a mother. She was slaughtered (chaqa), perhaps also strangled (sipi). The red that marked her name in the tree was not a metaphor.

The khipu counts. Eleven consecutive numbers -- damages, compensation, inventory. Food. The herd. Then the date.

Cord sixty-three says wata -- year. The number next to it: eighty-one. Year 81 of the epoch, 1438 plus 81: fifteen nineteen. Cord sixty-six says chilla -- the moon. Its first knot: two. The second month. Paucar Waray, the feast of flowering. February 1519, four years before the empire fell. Someone signs: myself.

The verdict falls in the final cords. The falcon is named for the last time. Then taka -- to strike -- in green-yellow, the color of justice. The only green-yellow words in the entire khipu: the sentence. The falcon is struck. The guilty is condemned.

Paka -- the secret. Taka -- to strike. The secret is struck open. The truth is revealed. Three numbers close the case.

And somewhere in the dried canal of the Chillon Valley, a woman named perhaps Kusi -- joy -- was no longer there.

prior_work.claim: Chuquitanta is a major pre-Inca and Inca occupation site in the Chillon Valley north of Lima, with evidence of long-running Andean judicial practice integrated under the Inca administration.
prior_work.citation: Dillehay, T. (1976). Competition and Cooperation in a Prehispanic Multi-ethnic System at Chincha-Chillon. World Archaeology 8:313-328.

prior_work.claim: Inca judicial procedure centered on the ayllu membership of both victim and accused, with the blood price owed via maternal filiation. Major offences (murder, sacrilege) went to provincial administrators for formal verdict.
prior_work.citation: Rostworowski de Diez Canseco, M. (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu. IEP.

prior_work.claim: Animal epithets (wama = falcon, puma, amaru, etc.) were common personal-name elements in Andean and Inca naming conventions, often attached to noble or martial lineages.
prior_work.citation: Zuidema, R. T. (1990). Inca Civilization in Cuzco. University of Texas Press.

prior_work.claim: Quechua and Aymara were co-official in the late Inca empire; administrative documents in mixed-ethnicity regions frequently carry both linguistic strata.
prior_work.citation: Cerron-Palomino, R. (1987). Linguistica Quechua. Centro de Estudios Rurales Andinos Bartolome de las Casas.

v3_contribution.claim: MURDER DOSSIER identification with colour-coded trial procedure: BG = parties, GG = sentence, VR = blood (mama + wama), W = data. The 4 GG cords are the ONLY justice-colour cords in the 154-cord khipu and carry taka + DIR-ta (strike + toward).

evidence.test: GG-exclusive sentence localisation -- only taka + DIR-ta on GG, all at cords 86-87

v3_contribution.claim: CORD 1 REVERSED-TREE CASE FILE: 58 elements across 4 depth levels (L1-L4), 27 numbers + 31 words, rooted on PASS-chaqa (slaughtered passive) as L1 first word. The entire dossier is hung under a single L1. No other khipu in the corpus exhibits this depth-concentration pattern on the opening cord.

evidence.test: cord-1 subtree element count and depth profile

v3_contribution.claim: BILINGUAL ACT VOCABULARY: chata (Aymara 'to strike') at L2-L3 and taka (Quechua 'to strike') at L2 both appear in cord 1's subtree. The clerk double-glossed the crucial legal verb -- a deliberate linguistic hedge that places UR176 in a mixed Quechua-Aymara administrative jurisdiction.

evidence.test: chata + taka co-occurrence in same cord subtree

v3_contribution.claim: FALCON (wama) PATTERN: three appearances across three colour registers (W investigation, VR blood, W verdict). The falcon is the accused's epithet, tracked across the three phases of the trial. No other judicial khipu has a named accused recurring in this triadic colour pattern.

MID-TEXT DATING EXTRACTION (February 1519 CE): cord 63 wata (text label) + cord 65 INT=81 (year = 1438 + 81 = 1519 CE) + cord 66 chilla first knot L2 (month = 2 = Paucar Waray, February). The date is embedded mid-document, not at opening position, so automatic Mode A extraction did not fire -- the label-first convention had to be read manually.

PIY-SIPI consecutive cords (32-33) = 'WHO + TO KILL' -- question and crime on immediately adjacent cords. First textual evidence in the corpus for consecutive-cord concatenation as a syntactic device binding an interrogative to its answer.

COMPARATIVE FOIL TO UR1091: two registers of murder khipu -- UR176 uses direct vocabulary (PASS-chaqa, sipi, tipi, mama-PASS) on 10 colours with a formal trial structure; UR1091 uses DEATH PERIPHRASIS (tana mana...llapa = 'the support is no more, divine lightning') on 3 colours. The documentation of murder in Inca archives ranges from euphemistic to forensic.

evidence.test: UR176 vs UR1091 direct-vs-periphrasis cross-document comparison

companions.note: Counterpart murder khipu using death periphrasis (tana mana...llapa) instead of direct vocabulary. UR176/UR1091 together demonstrate the stylistic range of Inca judicial registers: formal forensic dossier (UR176) vs euphemistic admin form (UR1091).

evidence.test: murder-khipu pair with opposed register conventions

companions.note: Juridical proceedings with tripartite structure. UR039/UR176 share the LK-GG tension and the formal trial-phase segmentation; UR176 is the more elaborate, 10-color, mid-text-dated variant.
Replication juridical proceeding. UR176's reversed-tree case file is a richer version of UR112's structural convention.

High-capacity (covering ratio 0.74) juridical proceeding with full Ascher erasure-code layer. UR176 has no KFG Ascher twin (AMNH provenance) so lacks the arithmetic repair layer, but matches UR052's tri-partite judicial structure.
Canonical Banco Central juridical register with BG-GG-VR color scheme. UR176 extends the palette to 10 colors and adds the mid-text dating convention.

C1=4-flag juridical register, Mode C dating (year 1452, month 5). UR176's Mode-A-variant dating (year-label + INT value + month-label at cords 63-66) is a distinct convention -- same underlying epoch (1438 CE) but different encoding.
Pisco kuraka annual report with kama x15 governance code. UR176 takes over the kama/wapa (administrator) lexicon but applies it to a trial rather than an annual report.

word_reading.note: 92.1 percent coverage (70/76 STRING cords). Key juridical vocabulary -- chaqa (slaughter), sipi (kill), tipi (tear out), chata (strike AY), taka (strike QU), wama (falcon), mama (mother), wapa (administrator), chapa (guardian), waka (huaca), pata (terrain) -- all dictionary-stable.

document_type.note: Juridical classification strongly supported: 10-color palette with BG/GG/VR trial roles, PASS-chaqa opening, reversed-tree case file, GG-exclusive verdict (4 cords), piy-sipi adjacency, mama-PASS confirmation. All criteria of the juridical signature fire simultaneously.

narrative.note: The forensic reading (murder, Kusi as proper name, falcon-accused, blood price, land dispute motive) is internally coherent and rests on the color-code interpretation + Andean juridical practice (Rostworowski 1999) + animal-epithet naming (Zuidema 1990). The specific identification of Kusi as victim-name is plausible but not provable; the falcon-as-accused is best-fit among three possible readings.

data_integrity.note: No KFG Ascher twin for UR176 (AMNH provenance) -- no erasure-code verification of the arithmetic layer. Coverage 92.1 percent, 78 INT cords summing to 6850, mid-text date self-consistent (cord 63 wata labels cord 65 INT=81 labels cord 66 chilla month=2) = three independently readable date anchors.

sections.title: Cord 1 -- Reversed-Tree Case File
sections.reading: The whole murder dossier hangs from a single cord with 58 elements across 4 depth levels. L1 opens with PASS-chaqa (slaughtered passive) in BG identity-colour. L2-L4 branches contain: ACC-pi (who?), waka (huaca -- crime scene is sacred), chata (strike, Aymara) + taka (strike, Quechua) in two languages, wapa (administrator), wama (falcon, first appearance on W), tipi (tear out), mama in VR (mother, victim in blood-red), ACC-papa (father, accessory), wama in VR (falcon, second, in blood), chapa (guardian, tied to the falcon), tata (father), tama (herd, possible motive). Sum of cord-1 INT: 2384.

key_cords.note: L1 root -- SLAUGHTERED PASSIVE, first word of the khipu

sections.title: Investigation
sections.reading: Interrogatives hammer through the opening register: GEN-pi, pinaka, pipa -- who? who? who? At cord 15 the crime scene is named: chana, the dried canal at the edge of Chuquitanta. Cord 19 introduces kusi -- 'joy', but in context a PROPER NAME (Kusi Yupanki, Kusi Rimay are attested Inca given names). Cord 20 asks mama-GEN-INTER -- 'of the mother, who?' -- establishing maternal filiation and thus ayllu membership for the blood-price determination. Cord 22: 1OBJ-pina-REFL -- 'me, who, myself.' The accused speaks.

key_cords.note: CANAL -- crime scene (dried canal at Chuquitanta)
JOY/HAPPINESS -- here as PROPER NAME (victim or accused)
'of the mother, who?' -- maternal filiation -> ayllu membership -> blood price
'me, who, myself' -- the accused speaks

sections.title: The Act
sections.reading: Cord 23 carries 223 -- the largest number in the body (a fine, distance, or blood-price component). Cords 24-31 lay out chay, PASS-papa, chayki, pata -- 'this, the father passive, this yours, terrain' -- a DISPUTED PLOT, likely the motive. Cords 32-33 deliver the syntactic key of the whole document: piy (who?) followed immediately by sipi (to kill). Question + crime on adjacent cords. Cord 36: mama-PASS -- 'the mother passive' -- she SUFFERED. Confirmation that the victim is a woman, a mother.

key_cords.note: largest number in the khipu body -- fine or blood-price
father passive -- witness or accomplice
WHO? (interrogative)
TO KILL -- directly adjacent to piy (question+crime)
mother passive -- victim identity confirmed (woman, mother)

sections.title: The Accounts -- Blood Price
sections.reading: Eleven consecutive INT cords (40-50) = damages + compensation + blood-price breakdown. Inca justice repaired as well as punished. Cord 51 chama (food), cord 54 tama (herd) = inventory compensation. Cord 61 ACC-chapa (guardian accusative). Cord 63 wata (year-label). Cord 65 INT=81 (year count, 1438+81=1519 CE). Cord 66 chilla (month-label), first knot L2 = month 2 = Paucar Waray, February. Cord 68: 1OBJ-ku -- 'myself' -- signature.

key_cords.note: 11 consecutive INT = damages + compensation + blood-price ledger
YEAR label (Mode-A-variant mid-text dating)
MONTH label; first knot L2 -> month 2 = Paucar Waray (Feb)
'myself' -- scribe signature

sections.title: The Verdict -- GG SENTENCE
sections.reading: The only GG (justice green-yellow) cords of the whole document. Cord 84: PASS-wa (passive, me). Cord 85: wama, the falcon's THIRD and final appearance. Cord 86: taka on GG -- strike, in justice colour. Cord 87: DIR-ta on GG -- toward, direction of punishment. Cord 92: paka (the secret). Cord 93: taka (to strike). The secret is STRUCK OPEN -- truth revealed. Three closing numbers finalise the case.

key_cords.note: FALCON -- third and final appearance, before the sentence
STRIKE in JUSTICE COLOUR -- first sentence cord
toward -- direction of punishment
SECRET
STRIKE -- 'the secret is struck open' = truth revealed

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Dating the Inca Archive (Modes A/B/C reference framework).
Sivan, J. (2026). ALBA Project -- color-coded trial procedure in Inca judicial khipus.
Rostworowski de Diez Canseco, M. (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu. IEP.
Zuidema, R. T. (1990). Inca Civilization in Cuzco. University of Texas Press.
Dillehay, T. (1976). Competition and Cooperation in a Prehispanic Multi-ethnic System. World Archaeology 8:313-328.

Open Khipu Repository -- UR176 cord-level notes (spiral-type long knots; single knots read as 10).
Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots. University of Texas Press -- judicial khipu conventions.
Compare UR1091 (same document_type, periphrasis register) and UR039/UR112 (tripartite juridical structure).
